pathways.title: Pre-Calculus Fundamentals
pathways.description: Build the essential math foundation needed before calculus. Master functions, trigonometry, exponentials, sequences, and more so you are fully prepared for Calculus I.

chapters.title: Functions and Graphs
chapters.description: Learn what functions are, how to read and build their graphs, and how to work with all the major function families you will encounter throughout calculus.

content_blocks.text: A function is a rule that takes an input and produces exactly one output. Think of it like a machine: you feed a number in, the machine does something to it, and one number comes out. If you put the same number in twice, you always get the same result.

We write functions using notation like f(x) = 2x + 3. Here, f is the name of the function and x is the input. If you plug in x = 4, you get f(4) = 2(4) + 3 = 11. The key rule is: every input gives exactly one output. A single input can never produce two different outputs.
Suppose f(x) = x^2. Then f(3) = 9, f(-3) = 9, and f(0) = 0. Notice that different inputs can give the same output (3 and -3 both give 9), but each input only gives one output.

The vertical line test is a quick way to check if a graph represents a function. If any vertical line crosses the graph more than once, it is not a function because that x-value would have two outputs.

Function notation is the standard way we write and talk about functions. When we write f(x) = 3x - 1, we are saying: the function named f takes input x and returns 3x - 1. The letter f is just a name — we could also call it g, h, or anything else.

Evaluating a function means substituting a specific value for x and computing the result. For f(x) = 3x - 1, evaluating at x = 5 means computing f(5) = 3(5) - 1 = 14. You can also evaluate at expressions: f(a + 1) = 3(a + 1) - 1 = 3a + 3 - 1 = 3a + 2.
Let g(x) = x^2 - 4x + 7. Find g(2). Substitute 2 for every x: g(2) = (2)^2 - 4(2) + 7 = 4 - 8 + 7 = 3.
Be careful with negative inputs. For f(x) = x^2, computing f(-3) means (-3)^2 = 9, not -(3^2) = -9. Always wrap the substituted value in parentheses to avoid sign errors.

The domain of a function is the set of all inputs (x-values) the function can accept. The range is the set of all outputs (y-values) the function can produce. Together, domain and range tell you the full scope of what a function can do.

Some functions accept any real number as input. For example, f(x) = 2x + 1 works for every x, so its domain is all real numbers. Other functions have restrictions. For f(x) = 1/x, you cannot use x = 0 because division by zero is undefined. So the domain is all real numbers except 0.

For f(x) = sqrt(x), the input must be zero or positive because you cannot take the square root of a negative number (in real numbers). So the domain is x >= 0. The range of sqrt(x) is y >= 0 because square roots are never negative.
Find the domain of f(x) = 1/(x - 3). The denominator cannot be zero, so x - 3 ≠ 0, meaning x ≠ 3. The domain is all real numbers except x = 3.

A linear function has the form f(x) = mx + b, where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept. Its graph is always a straight line. Linear functions are the simplest type of function and serve as a building block for understanding all others.

The slope m tells you how steep the line is and which direction it tilts. A positive slope means the line rises from left to right. A negative slope means it falls. A slope of zero gives a perfectly horizontal line. The y-intercept b is where the line crosses the y-axis (the point (0, b)).

Slope is calculated as the change in y divided by the change in x between any two points: m = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1). This ratio is constant everywhere on a straight line, which is exactly what makes the function linear — it changes at a constant rate.
The line through (1, 2) and (4, 8) has slope m = (8 - 2)/(4 - 1) = 6/3 = 2. Using point-slope form: y - 2 = 2(x - 1), which simplifies to y = 2x. The y-intercept is 0.

slides.title: Worked Example: Analyzing a Linear Function

content_blocks.text: A phone plan charges a $20 monthly fee plus $0.05 per text message. Write a function for the monthly cost C(t) where t is the number of texts. Find the cost for 200 texts. What is the slope and what does it mean?
Step 1: Identify the structure. The cost has a fixed part ($20) plus a variable part ($0.05 per text). This is linear: C(t) = 0.05t + 20.
Step 2: Evaluate at t = 200. C(200) = 0.05(200) + 20 = 10 + 20 = 30. The monthly cost for 200 texts is $30.

slides.title: Worked Example: Analyzing a Linear Function

content_blocks.text: Step 3: Identify the slope. The slope is 0.05. It means each additional text message adds $0.05 to the bill. The slope is the rate of change of cost with respect to the number of texts.
Step 4: Identify the y-intercept. The y-intercept is 20. It represents the base cost when t = 0 (no texts sent). You still pay $20 just for having the plan.

A quadratic function has the form f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c, where a ≠ 0. Its graph is a U-shaped curve called a parabola. If a is positive, the parabola opens upward (like a bowl). If a is negative, it opens downward (like an upside-down bowl).

The most important point on a parabola is the vertex — the peak or valley of the curve. The vertex is located at x = -b/(2a). Plugging this x back into f(x) gives you the y-coordinate of the vertex. The vertical line through the vertex is the axis of symmetry: the parabola is a mirror image on either side.

The zeros (or roots) of a quadratic are the x-values where f(x) = 0. You can find them by factoring, completing the square, or using the quadratic formula: x = (-b ± sqrt(b^2 - 4ac))/(2a). The expression b^2 - 4ac is called the discriminant. If it is positive, there are two real zeros. If zero, there is one. If negative, there are no real zeros.
For f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 8, the vertex is at x = -(-6)/(2·1) = 3, giving f(3) = 9 - 18 + 8 = -1. The vertex is (3, -1). Setting f(x) = 0: x^2 - 6x + 8 = (x-2)(x-4) = 0, so zeros are x = 2 and x = 4.

A polynomial function is a sum of terms where each term is a constant times x raised to a whole-number power. Examples include f(x) = x^3 - 2x + 5 and g(x) = 4x^5 - x^2 + 3. The highest power of x is called the degree of the polynomial. Linear functions have degree 1, quadratics have degree 2, cubics have degree 3, and so on.

The degree tells you a lot about the graph's behavior. A polynomial of degree n can have at most n real zeros (x-intercepts) and at most n - 1 turning points (peaks and valleys). The end behavior — what happens as x goes to positive or negative infinity — depends on the leading term (the highest-degree term).

For example, if the leading term is x^3, then as x goes to positive infinity, f(x) also goes to positive infinity, and as x goes to negative infinity, f(x) goes to negative infinity. If the leading term is -x^4, the graph falls on both ends because the negative sign flips the curve and even-degree polynomials behave the same on both sides.
f(x) = x^3 - 3x has degree 3. Its zeros: x(x^2 - 3) = 0 gives x = 0, x = sqrt(3), x = -sqrt(3). It has at most 2 turning points. End behavior: falls to the left, rises to the right.

A rational function is a ratio of two polynomials: f(x) = P(x)/Q(x). For example, f(x) = (x + 1)/(x - 2). These functions behave differently from polynomials because the denominator can equal zero, creating gaps or blow-ups in the graph.

A vertical asymptote occurs at any x-value that makes the denominator zero (and does not cancel with the numerator). The graph shoots up or down toward infinity near these points. For f(x) = (x+1)/(x-2), there is a vertical asymptote at x = 2.

A horizontal asymptote describes what happens to f(x) as x gets very large or very small. If the degree of the numerator equals the degree of the denominator, the horizontal asymptote is the ratio of their leading coefficients. If the numerator degree is less, the horizontal asymptote is y = 0. If the numerator degree is greater, there is no horizontal asymptote (but there may be an oblique one).
For f(x) = (2x + 1)/(x - 3): vertical asymptote at x = 3. Both numerator and denominator have degree 1, so horizontal asymptote is y = 2/1 = 2. As x gets huge, f(x) approaches 2.

slides.title: Worked Example: Analyzing a Rational Function

content_blocks.text: Find the domain, vertical asymptote(s), and horizontal asymptote of f(x) = (3x)/(x^2 - 4). Describe what happens near each asymptote.
Step 1: Find the domain. Set the denominator equal to zero: x^2 - 4 = 0, so (x-2)(x+2) = 0, giving x = 2 and x = -2. The domain is all real numbers except x = 2 and x = -2.

Step 2: Check for vertical asymptotes. The numerator 3x does not equal zero at x = 2 or x = -2 (it equals 6 and -6 respectively), so nothing cancels. There are vertical asymptotes at x = 2 and x = -2.

slides.title: Worked Example: Analyzing a Rational Function

content_blocks.text: Step 3: Find the horizontal asymptote. The numerator has degree 1 and the denominator has degree 2. Since the denominator degree is larger, the horizontal asymptote is y = 0. As x gets very large, f(x) approaches 0.

Step 4: Describe behavior near asymptotes. Near x = 2, the function blows up to positive infinity on one side and negative infinity on the other. The same happens near x = -2. The graph gets closer and closer to y = 0 as you move far left or far right.

Once you know the graph of a basic function, you can create many new graphs by applying transformations. There are four main types: vertical shifts, horizontal shifts, vertical stretches or compressions, and reflections.

Vertical shift: f(x) + k moves the graph up by k units (down if k is negative). Horizontal shift: f(x - h) moves the graph right by h units (left if h is negative). Notice the sign is opposite — f(x - 3) shifts right, not left.

Vertical stretch/compression: a·f(x) stretches the graph vertically by factor a if |a| > 1, or compresses it if |a| < 1. If a is negative, the graph also flips over the x-axis. Horizontal stretch/compression: f(bx) compresses horizontally by factor b if |b| > 1.

Start with f(x) = x^2. Then g(x) = (x - 2)^2 + 3 is the same parabola shifted 2 units right and 3 units up. The vertex moves from (0, 0) to (2, 3). And h(x) = -x^2 is the parabola flipped upside-down.

Function composition means using the output of one function as the input of another. If f(x) = 2x and g(x) = x + 3, then the composition (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = f(x + 3) = 2(x + 3) = 2x + 6. You evaluate the inner function first, then feed its result into the outer function.
The order matters: (f ∘ g)(x) is usually different from (g ∘ f)(x). Using the same functions: (g ∘ f)(x) = g(f(x)) = g(2x) = 2x + 3, which is not the same as 2x + 6.

An inverse function undoes what the original function does. If f(x) = 2x + 3, its inverse f^(-1)(x) reverses the process: subtract 3, then divide by 2, giving f^(-1)(x) = (x - 3)/2. Composing a function with its inverse always returns the original input: f(f^(-1)(x)) = x.

Not every function has an inverse. A function must be one-to-one (each output comes from exactly one input) to have an inverse. The horizontal line test checks this: if any horizontal line crosses the graph more than once, the function is not one-to-one.
1) For f(x) = (x - 1)/(x + 4), find the domain, the vertical asymptote, and the horizontal asymptote.
2) Let g(x) = x^2 + 2x - 8. Find the vertex, the axis of symmetry, and the zeros of g.
3) Given f(x) = 3x - 5 and g(x) = x^2, compute (f ∘ g)(2) and (g ∘ f)(2).
4) Describe the transformations applied to f(x) = |x| to obtain g(x) = -2|x - 1| + 4.
A function assigns exactly one output to each input. We use f(x) notation to name and evaluate functions. Every function has a domain (allowed inputs) and range (possible outputs).

Linear functions (y = mx + b) have constant rate of change and straight-line graphs. Quadratic functions (y = ax^2 + bx + c) form parabolas with a vertex and axis of symmetry. Polynomials generalize these with higher degrees, and their end behavior is controlled by the leading term.

Rational functions (ratios of polynomials) introduce asymptotes — lines the graph approaches but never crosses. Vertical asymptotes occur where the denominator is zero; horizontal asymptotes describe long-run behavior.

Transformations (shifts, stretches, reflections) let you build new graphs from familiar ones. Composition chains functions together, and inverse functions undo each other. These core ideas will appear constantly in calculus.

chapters.title: Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
chapters.description: Understand exponential growth and decay, learn what logarithms are and why they matter, and practice solving equations involving these powerful function families.

content_blocks.text: An exponential function has the form f(x) = a · b^x, where a is the starting value and b is the base. The base b must be positive and not equal to 1. What makes exponentials special is that the variable x is in the exponent, not the base.

This creates a distinctive growth pattern. Instead of adding a fixed amount each step (like linear functions), exponential functions multiply by a fixed factor each step. If b > 1, the function grows — slowly at first, then faster and faster. If 0 < b < 1, the function decays, shrinking toward zero.

The graph of an exponential function always passes through (0, a) because b^0 = 1 for any base b, so f(0) = a · 1 = a. The graph has a horizontal asymptote at y = 0: the function gets close to zero but never actually reaches it (for decay) or rises from near zero (for growth).
f(x) = 3 · 2^x starts at f(0) = 3. At x = 1 it is 6, at x = 2 it is 12, at x = 3 it is 24. Each step doubles the previous value. That is the power of exponential growth.

Exponential functions model countless real-world situations. When a quantity increases by a fixed percentage each time period, it grows exponentially. When it decreases by a fixed percentage, it decays exponentially.

Population growth: if a town of 10,000 grows by 3% per year, the population after t years is P(t) = 10000 · (1.03)^t. Radioactive decay: if a substance loses half its mass every 5 years (half-life = 5), the remaining mass after t years is M(t) = M_0 · (0.5)^(t/5).

Compound interest is another classic example. If you invest P dollars at an annual rate r compounded n times per year, after t years you have A = P · (1 + r/n)^(nt). As n gets larger and larger (compounding more frequently), this approaches A = P · e^(rt), which uses continuous compounding.
Exponential growth is deceptively fast. A population growing at just 2% per year will double in about 35 years. Understanding this is important for biology, finance, and many other fields.

Among all possible bases for exponential functions, one stands out: the number e, approximately 2.71828. It is called Euler's number and appears naturally whenever growth or decay happens continuously.

Where does e come from? Imagine investing $1 at 100% annual interest. Compounded once: you get $2. Compounded twice (50% each half-year): $2.25. Compounded monthly: about $2.61. Compounded every second: about $2.71828. As you compound more and more frequently, the result approaches e. Formally, e = limit of (1 + 1/n)^n as n approaches infinity.

The function f(x) = e^x is called the natural exponential function. It has a remarkable property that will become central in calculus: its rate of change at any point equals its value at that point. This makes e the most natural base for modeling continuous processes.

Continuous growth model: a colony of bacteria doubles every hour. Using the continuous model, the count is N(t) = N_0 · e^(kt), where k = ln(2) ≈ 0.693. So if N_0 = 100, after 3 hours: N(3) = 100 · e^(0.693·3) ≈ 100 · 8 = 800.
A car worth $25,000 depreciates by 15% each year. Write a function for its value V(t) after t years. What is it worth after 4 years? When will it be worth less than $10,000?
Step 1: Set up the model. Each year the car keeps 85% of its value (100% - 15% = 85%). So V(t) = 25000 · (0.85)^t.

Step 3: Solve 25000 · (0.85)^t < 10000. Divide both sides by 25000: (0.85)^t < 0.4. Take the natural log of both sides: t · ln(0.85) < ln(0.4). Since ln(0.85) is negative, dividing flips the inequality: t > ln(0.4)/ln(0.85) ≈ (-0.9163)/(-0.1625) ≈ 5.64.
Step 4: Interpret. The car drops below $10,000 after about 5.64 years, so partway through the 6th year.
A logarithm answers the question: what exponent do I need? Specifically, log_b(x) = y means b^y = x. In words, log base b of x is the power you raise b to in order to get x.

For example, log_2(8) = 3 because 2^3 = 8. And log_10(1000) = 3 because 10^3 = 1000. Logarithms are the inverse of exponential functions — they undo exponentiation, just like subtraction undoes addition.

The graph of y = log_b(x) is the reflection of y = b^x over the line y = x. It passes through (1, 0) because b^0 = 1 for any base. The domain is x > 0 (you can only take the log of a positive number) and the range is all real numbers. There is a vertical asymptote at x = 0.

Logarithms have three key properties that come directly from the rules of exponents. These properties turn multiplication into addition, division into subtraction, and powers into multiplication — which is extremely useful for simplifying and solving equations.

Product rule: log_b(A · B) = log_b(A) + log_b(B). The log of a product is the sum of the logs. Quotient rule: log_b(A/B) = log_b(A) - log_b(B). The log of a quotient is the difference of the logs. Power rule: log_b(A^n) = n · log_b(A). The log of a power brings the exponent down as a multiplier.
These properties work because exponents add when you multiply same-base powers: b^m · b^n = b^(m+n). Since logs are exponents, the same addition relationship holds.
Expand log_2(8x^3). Using the product rule: log_2(8) + log_2(x^3). Then the power rule: 3 + 3·log_2(x). So log_2(8x^3) = 3 + 3·log_2(x).

Two bases are used so often they get special names and shorthand. The common logarithm uses base 10 and is written simply as log(x) (no base written). The natural logarithm uses base e and is written as ln(x).

Common logs are handy for everyday scales. The Richter scale for earthquakes, the decibel scale for sound, and pH in chemistry all use log base 10. Going up by 1 on these scales means multiplying the actual quantity by 10.

Natural logs appear throughout calculus and science because they pair with the natural exponential e^x. The key relationships are: ln(e^x) = x and e^(ln x) = x. These show that ln and e^x are perfect inverses of each other.
The change of base formula lets you convert between any two bases: log_b(x) = ln(x)/ln(b) = log(x)/log(b). This is useful when your calculator only has log and ln buttons.

slides.title: Worked Example: Solving a Logarithmic Equation

content_blocks.text: Solve for x: log_3(2x + 1) = 4.
Step 1: Convert from logarithmic to exponential form. log_3(2x + 1) = 4 means 3^4 = 2x + 1.
Step 2: Compute 3^4 = 81. So the equation becomes 81 = 2x + 1.
Step 3: Solve for x. Subtract 1: 80 = 2x. Divide by 2: x = 40.

slides.title: Worked Example: Solving a Logarithmic Equation

content_blocks.text: Step 4: Check. log_3(2(40) + 1) = log_3(81) = log_3(3^4) = 4. Confirmed.

Exponential functions and logarithmic functions are inverses of each other. This means they undo each other. If f(x) = b^x, then f^(-1)(x) = log_b(x). Graphically, the log curve is the exponential curve reflected over the line y = x.
This inverse relationship gives us two cancellation identities: b^(log_b(x)) = x for all x > 0, and log_b(b^x) = x for all real x. These are incredibly useful for solving equations.

To solve an exponential equation like 5^x = 200, take log base 5 of both sides: x = log_5(200). Using change of base: x = ln(200)/ln(5) ≈ 5.292/1.609 ≈ 3.29. To solve a log equation like ln(x) = 7, exponentiate both sides with base e: x = e^7 ≈ 1096.6.
When you see an equation with the variable in the exponent, think 'take a log.' When the variable is inside a log, think 'exponentiate.' These inverse operations are your main tools.

Many real-world problems require solving equations involving exponentials or logs. The strategy depends on where the variable appears. If x is in the exponent, isolate the exponential expression and take a logarithm of both sides. If x is inside a logarithm, isolate the log and convert to exponential form.
Solve 4 · e^(0.3t) = 100. First isolate: e^(0.3t) = 25. Take ln of both sides: 0.3t = ln(25) ≈ 3.219. Divide: t ≈ 10.73.
Solve 2·log(x) + log(3) = log(75). Use log properties: log(x^2) + log(3) = log(75), so log(3x^2) = log(75). Therefore 3x^2 = 75, x^2 = 25, x = 5 (reject -5 since log requires positive input).
Always check your answers in the original equation. Logarithms are only defined for positive inputs, so reject any solution that makes the argument of a log zero or negative.
1) A bank account has $5,000 growing at 4% per year compounded annually. Write the function A(t) and find the balance after 10 years.
2) Evaluate without a calculator: log_4(64), ln(e^5), log(0.001).
3) Use log properties to expand: ln(x^2 · sqrt(y) / z^3).

Exponential functions f(x) = a · b^x model quantities that grow or decay by a fixed percentage per step. The base b determines the behavior: b > 1 gives growth, 0 < b < 1 gives decay. The number e ≈ 2.718 is the natural base for continuous processes.

Logarithms are the inverses of exponentials: log_b(x) answers 'what power of b gives x?' The three key log properties (product, quotient, power) turn complex expressions into simpler ones. Common logs (base 10) and natural logs (base e) are the most widely used.

To solve exponential equations, take a log. To solve log equations, exponentiate. These inverse operations are the central strategy. Always check that solutions keep log arguments positive. These functions and skills will be essential when you study derivatives and integrals of exponentials and logs in calculus.

chapters.title: Trigonometry
chapters.description: Master angles, the unit circle, sine, cosine, tangent, trig identities, and graphing trig functions — skills you will use constantly in calculus.

content_blocks.text: An angle measures the amount of rotation from one direction to another. You are probably used to measuring angles in degrees, where a full rotation is 360 degrees. In calculus and advanced math, we use a different unit called radians.

One radian is the angle created when the arc length along a circle equals the radius of that circle. Since the circumference of a circle is 2·pi·r, a full rotation is 2·pi radians. So 360 degrees = 2·pi radians, which means 180 degrees = pi radians.

To convert: multiply degrees by pi/180 to get radians, or multiply radians by 180/pi to get degrees. Key angles to memorize: 30° = pi/6, 45° = pi/4, 60° = pi/3, 90° = pi/2, 180° = pi, 270° = 3·pi/2, 360° = 2·pi.
Calculus almost always uses radians. The derivative formulas for sine and cosine only work cleanly in radians. Get comfortable thinking in radians now — it will save you headaches later.
The unit circle is a circle with radius 1 centered at the origin. It is the key tool for defining trigonometric functions for any angle, not just angles in triangles.

Start at the point (1, 0) on the right side of the circle. As you move counterclockwise by an angle theta, you arrive at a point on the circle. The x-coordinate of that point is cos(theta) and the y-coordinate is sin(theta). This simple idea defines sine and cosine for all angles.

Since the circle has radius 1, every point satisfies x^2 + y^2 = 1, which gives us the fundamental identity: cos^2(theta) + sin^2(theta) = 1. This identity is true for every angle and will appear throughout calculus.
At theta = pi/3 (60°), the point on the unit circle is (1/2, sqrt(3)/2). So cos(pi/3) = 1/2 and sin(pi/3) = sqrt(3)/2. Check: (1/2)^2 + (sqrt(3)/2)^2 = 1/4 + 3/4 = 1.

The three primary trig functions are sine, cosine, and tangent. From a right triangle perspective: for an acute angle theta in a right triangle, sin(theta) = opposite/hypotenuse, cos(theta) = adjacent/hypotenuse, and tan(theta) = opposite/adjacent. The mnemonic SOH-CAH-TOA helps you remember this.

Using the unit circle definition: sin(theta) is the y-coordinate, cos(theta) is the x-coordinate, and tan(theta) = sin(theta)/cos(theta) = y/x. Tangent is undefined whenever cos(theta) = 0 (at theta = pi/2, 3·pi/2, etc.).

There are also three reciprocal functions: csc(theta) = 1/sin(theta), sec(theta) = 1/cos(theta), and cot(theta) = 1/tan(theta) = cos(theta)/sin(theta). These show up in calculus integration problems regularly.
At theta = pi/4 (45°), the unit circle point is (sqrt(2)/2, sqrt(2)/2). So sin(pi/4) = sqrt(2)/2, cos(pi/4) = sqrt(2)/2, and tan(pi/4) = 1.
Find the exact values of sin(5·pi/6), cos(5·pi/6), and tan(5·pi/6).
Step 1: Find the reference angle. 5·pi/6 is in the second quadrant (between pi/2 and pi). The reference angle is pi - 5·pi/6 = pi/6.
Step 2: Recall the values at pi/6. sin(pi/6) = 1/2 and cos(pi/6) = sqrt(3)/2.
Step 3: Apply quadrant signs. In the second quadrant, sine is positive and cosine is negative. So sin(5·pi/6) = 1/2 and cos(5·pi/6) = -sqrt(3)/2.
Step 4: Compute tangent. tan(5·pi/6) = sin/cos = (1/2)/(-sqrt(3)/2) = -1/sqrt(3) = -sqrt(3)/3.

The graphs of y = sin(x) and y = cos(x) are smooth, wave-like curves that repeat every 2·pi units. This repeating behavior is called periodicity. The period is the length of one complete cycle: for both basic sine and cosine, the period is 2·pi.

Both graphs oscillate between -1 and 1. The amplitude is the distance from the center line (y = 0) to the peak, which is 1 for the basic functions. Sine starts at 0, rises to 1 at pi/2, returns to 0 at pi, drops to -1 at 3·pi/2, and returns to 0 at 2·pi. Cosine starts at 1, drops to 0 at pi/2, reaches -1 at pi, returns to 0 at 3·pi/2, and goes back to 1 at 2·pi.

For the general form y = A·sin(Bx - C) + D: |A| is the amplitude, 2·pi/|B| is the period, C/B is the phase shift (horizontal shift), and D is the vertical shift (midline). These parameters let you model any sinusoidal wave.
y = 3·sin(2x) has amplitude 3 and period 2·pi/2 = pi. It oscillates between -3 and 3, completing one full cycle every pi units instead of every 2·pi units.

slides.title: Graphs of Tangent and Other Trig Functions

content_blocks.text: The tangent function y = tan(x) has a very different shape from sine and cosine. It has vertical asymptotes at x = pi/2 + n·pi (where cosine is zero). Between each pair of asymptotes, the graph rises from negative infinity to positive infinity. Its period is pi, not 2·pi.

Cotangent y = cot(x) = cos(x)/sin(x) is similar but shifted: it has vertical asymptotes where sine is zero (x = n·pi) and its period is also pi. The graph falls from positive infinity to negative infinity between asymptotes.

Secant y = sec(x) = 1/cos(x) has U-shaped branches opening upward and downward, with vertical asymptotes where cosine is zero. Cosecant y = csc(x) = 1/sin(x) has a similar shape but with asymptotes where sine is zero. Both have period 2·pi.

slides.title: Graphs of Tangent and Other Trig Functions

content_blocks.text: You will encounter all six trig functions in calculus. Tangent and secant appear especially often in derivative and integration formulas, so getting comfortable with their graphs and behaviors now is worthwhile.

Trigonometric identities are equations that are true for all valid angles. They are not something to solve — they are facts you can always use to simplify or rewrite expressions. Mastering identities is one of the most important parts of pre-calculus.

Pythagorean identities: sin^2(theta) + cos^2(theta) = 1. Dividing through by cos^2 gives tan^2(theta) + 1 = sec^2(theta). Dividing through by sin^2 gives 1 + cot^2(theta) = csc^2(theta). These three identities come up constantly.

Reciprocal identities: csc = 1/sin, sec = 1/cos, cot = 1/tan. Quotient identities: tan = sin/cos, cot = cos/sin. Even-odd identities: cos(-theta) = cos(theta) (cosine is even), sin(-theta) = -sin(theta) (sine is odd), tan(-theta) = -tan(theta) (tangent is odd).
You do not need to memorize all identities blindly. Focus on sin^2 + cos^2 = 1, the reciprocal relationships, and tan = sin/cos. Many other identities can be derived from these.

slides.title: Sum, Difference, and Double Angle Formulas

content_blocks.text: The angle sum and difference formulas let you find the trig values of combined angles. sin(A + B) = sin(A)cos(B) + cos(A)sin(B). sin(A - B) = sin(A)cos(B) - cos(A)sin(B). cos(A + B) = cos(A)cos(B) - sin(A)sin(B). cos(A - B) = cos(A)cos(B) + sin(A)sin(B).

Setting A = B in the sum formulas gives the double angle formulas. sin(2A) = 2·sin(A)·cos(A). cos(2A) = cos^2(A) - sin^2(A), which can also be written as 2·cos^2(A) - 1 or 1 - 2·sin^2(A). tan(2A) = 2·tan(A)/(1 - tan^2(A)).

From the cos(2A) formulas, we get the half-angle or power-reduction formulas: cos^2(A) = (1 + cos(2A))/2 and sin^2(A) = (1 - cos(2A))/2. These are extremely useful in calculus for integrating sin^2(x) and cos^2(x).

slides.title: Sum, Difference, and Double Angle Formulas
Worked Example: Verifying a Trig Identity

content_blocks.text: Verify the identity: (1 - cos^2(x)) / sin(x) = sin(x).
Step 1: Focus on the left side. The numerator is 1 - cos^2(x). By the Pythagorean identity, sin^2(x) + cos^2(x) = 1, so 1 - cos^2(x) = sin^2(x).
Step 2: Substitute. The left side becomes sin^2(x) / sin(x).
Step 3: Simplify. sin^2(x) / sin(x) = sin(x). The left side equals the right side. Identity verified.

slides.title: Worked Example: Verifying a Trig Identity

content_blocks.text: When verifying identities, work on one side only and transform it into the other side. The Pythagorean identity is your most powerful tool — look for opportunities to substitute sin^2 + cos^2 = 1 in various rearranged forms.
Inverse trig functions answer the question: what angle gives this trig value? For example, arcsin(1/2) (also written sin^(-1)(1/2)) asks: what angle has a sine of 1/2? The answer is pi/6 (30°).

Since trig functions are periodic (they repeat), they are not one-to-one on their full domain. To define inverses, we restrict the domain. For arcsin (sin^(-1)), the output is in [-pi/2, pi/2]. For arccos (cos^(-1)), the output is in [0, pi]. For arctan (tan^(-1)), the output is in (-pi/2, pi/2).

These restricted ranges ensure each input gives exactly one output. Arcsin has domain [-1, 1], arccos has domain [-1, 1], and arctan has domain all real numbers (with horizontal asymptotes at y = pi/2 and y = -pi/2).
arccos(-1) = pi because cos(pi) = -1 and pi is in [0, pi]. arctan(1) = pi/4 because tan(pi/4) = 1 and pi/4 is in (-pi/2, pi/2).

A trig equation asks you to find the angle(s) that make a statement true. For example, solve sin(x) = 1/2. From the unit circle, x = pi/6 is one solution. But since sine is periodic, there are infinitely many solutions: x = pi/6 + 2·n·pi and x = 5·pi/6 + 2·n·pi for any integer n.

Most problems ask for solutions in a specific interval like [0, 2·pi). In that case, list only the solutions that fall in that range. For sin(x) = 1/2 on [0, 2·pi), the answers are x = pi/6 and x = 5·pi/6.

More complex trig equations may require algebraic manipulation first. Factor, use identities to rewrite in terms of a single function, or make a substitution. For example, 2·sin^2(x) - sin(x) - 1 = 0 can be factored as (2·sin(x) + 1)(sin(x) - 1) = 0, giving sin(x) = -1/2 or sin(x) = 1.
Solve cos(2x) = 0 on [0, 2·pi). First, 2x = pi/2 + n·pi. So x = pi/4 + n·pi/2. In [0, 2·pi), that gives x = pi/4, 3·pi/4, 5·pi/4, 7·pi/4.
1) Convert 225° to radians and find the exact values of sin(225°), cos(225°), and tan(225°).
2) Verify the identity: tan(x)·cos(x) = sin(x).
3) Find the amplitude, period, and phase shift of y = 2·cos(3x - pi).
4) Solve 2·cos(x) + 1 = 0 on [0, 2·pi).

Radians are the standard angle measure in calculus, with pi radians = 180 degrees. The unit circle defines sin(theta) as the y-coordinate and cos(theta) as the x-coordinate of a point on a circle of radius 1.

The Pythagorean identity sin^2 + cos^2 = 1 is the most fundamental relationship. The angle sum, double angle, and power-reduction formulas expand your ability to simplify and compute. Verifying identities strengthens your algebraic flexibility.

Trig graphs are periodic waves characterized by amplitude, period, and phase shift. Inverse trig functions (arcsin, arccos, arctan) reverse the process to find angles from values. Solving trig equations requires knowledge of the unit circle and careful attention to periodicity.
Trigonometry is woven throughout calculus. Derivatives of sin and cos, trig substitution for integrals, and Fourier analysis all depend on the concepts in this chapter.

chapters.title: Complex Numbers
chapters.description: Learn about the imaginary unit i, how to perform arithmetic with complex numbers, and how they connect to the complex plane and Euler's formula.

content_blocks.text: When you try to solve x^2 = -1, there is no real number that works because squaring any real number gives a positive result (or zero). To handle this, mathematicians invented the imaginary unit i, defined by i^2 = -1. In other words, i = sqrt(-1).
With i, you can take square roots of negative numbers. sqrt(-9) = sqrt(9) · sqrt(-1) = 3i. sqrt(-25) = 5i. This opens up a whole new number system that extends the real numbers.

Powers of i follow a repeating cycle of four: i^1 = i, i^2 = -1, i^3 = i^2 · i = -i, i^4 = (i^2)^2 = 1. Then it repeats: i^5 = i, i^6 = -1, and so on. To find any power of i, just divide the exponent by 4 and look at the remainder.
Find i^23. Divide 23 by 4: 23 = 4 · 5 + 3. The remainder is 3, so i^23 = i^3 = -i.

A complex number has the form a + bi, where a is the real part and b is the imaginary part. For example, 3 + 2i has real part 3 and imaginary part 2. The number 5 is also complex (5 + 0i), and so is 4i (0 + 4i).
Addition and subtraction: combine like parts. (3 + 2i) + (1 - 5i) = 4 - 3i. (7 + i) - (2 + 4i) = 5 - 3i. Just add the real parts together and the imaginary parts together.
Multiplication: use the distributive property (FOIL). (2 + 3i)(4 - i) = 8 - 2i + 12i - 3i^2 = 8 + 10i - 3(-1) = 8 + 10i + 3 = 11 + 10i. The key step is replacing i^2 with -1.
(1 + i)^2 = 1 + 2i + i^2 = 1 + 2i + (-1) = 2i. Squaring (1 + i) gives a purely imaginary number.

Complex numbers can be visualized on the complex plane (also called the Argand diagram). The horizontal axis represents the real part and the vertical axis represents the imaginary part. The number 3 + 2i is plotted at the point (3, 2).

The modulus (or absolute value) of a complex number a + bi is its distance from the origin: |a + bi| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2). This is just the distance formula from the Pythagorean theorem. For example, |3 + 4i| = sqrt(9 + 16) = 5.

The argument of a complex number is the angle it makes with the positive real axis, measured counterclockwise. For 3 + 4i, the argument is arctan(4/3). Together, modulus and argument give the polar form of a complex number.

The complex plane gives geometry to numbers. Adding complex numbers corresponds to vector addition. Multiplying by i rotates a point 90 degrees counterclockwise. This geometric view becomes powerful in advanced math and engineering.
Let z = 2 - 3i and w = 1 + 4i. Find z + w, z · w, and |z|.
Step 3: Modulus of z. |z| = |2 - 3i| = sqrt(2^2 + (-3)^2) = sqrt(4 + 9) = sqrt(13) ≈ 3.606.
The complex conjugate of a + bi is a - bi. You flip the sign of the imaginary part. For example, the conjugate of 3 + 2i is 3 - 2i. The conjugate of -1 - 5i is -1 + 5i.

Conjugates have a useful property: when you multiply a complex number by its conjugate, the result is always a real number. (a + bi)(a - bi) = a^2 - (bi)^2 = a^2 + b^2. This is because i^2 = -1 makes the cross terms cancel.

This property is the key to dividing complex numbers. To compute (3 + 2i)/(1 - i), multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator: (3 + 2i)(1 + i) / ((1 - i)(1 + i)) = (3 + 3i + 2i + 2i^2) / (1 + 1) = (3 + 5i - 2) / 2 = (1 + 5i) / 2 = 1/2 + 5i/2.
Multiplying by the conjugate to clear complex denominators is the same idea as rationalizing the denominator with radicals. It is a technique you will use often.

Instead of writing a complex number as a + bi (rectangular form), you can describe it using its modulus r and argument theta: z = r(cos(theta) + i·sin(theta)). This is called polar form. Here r = |z| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2) and theta = arctan(b/a) (adjusted for the correct quadrant).

Polar form makes multiplication and division beautifully simple. To multiply two complex numbers in polar form, multiply their moduli and add their arguments: r1·(cos theta1 + i·sin theta1) times r2·(cos theta2 + i·sin theta2) = r1·r2·(cos(theta1 + theta2) + i·sin(theta1 + theta2)).

This also makes computing powers easy. De Moivre's Theorem says: [r(cos theta + i·sin theta)]^n = r^n · (cos(n·theta) + i·sin(n·theta)). To raise a complex number to the nth power, raise the modulus to the nth power and multiply the angle by n.

Convert 1 + i to polar form. r = sqrt(1 + 1) = sqrt(2). theta = arctan(1/1) = pi/4. So 1 + i = sqrt(2) · (cos(pi/4) + i·sin(pi/4)). Then (1+i)^8 = (sqrt(2))^8 · (cos(8·pi/4) + i·sin(8·pi/4)) = 16 · (cos(2·pi) + i·sin(2·pi)) = 16.

slides.title: Worked Example: Converting to Polar Form

content_blocks.text: Write z = -1 + sqrt(3)·i in polar form. Then use De Moivre's Theorem to find z^3.
Step 1: Find the modulus. r = sqrt((-1)^2 + (sqrt(3))^2) = sqrt(1 + 3) = sqrt(4) = 2.
Step 2: Find the argument. arctan(sqrt(3)/(-1)) gives a reference angle of pi/3. Since the real part is negative and imaginary part is positive, we are in quadrant II. So theta = pi - pi/3 = 2·pi/3.
Step 3: Write polar form. z = 2(cos(2·pi/3) + i·sin(2·pi/3)).

slides.title: Worked Example: Converting to Polar Form

content_blocks.text: Step 4: Apply De Moivre's Theorem for z^3. z^3 = 2^3 · (cos(3 · 2·pi/3) + i·sin(3 · 2·pi/3)) = 8 · (cos(2·pi) + i·sin(2·pi)) = 8 · (1 + 0i) = 8.

One of the most beautiful results in all of mathematics connects exponentials, trigonometry, and complex numbers. Euler's formula states: e^(i·theta) = cos(theta) + i·sin(theta). This means the polar form of a complex number can be written as z = r · e^(i·theta).

This formula is not obvious — it will be justified in calculus using Taylor series. But even now, it is worth appreciating. It says that raising e to an imaginary power gives a point on the unit circle in the complex plane. The angle theta determines where on the circle you land.

A famous special case is Euler's identity: e^(i·pi) + 1 = 0. This single equation links five of the most important constants in math: e, i, pi, 1, and 0. Many consider it the most elegant equation ever discovered.

You do not need to fully understand why Euler's formula works yet. Just know it exists and that it creates a deep bridge between exponentials and trig. You will see it again when studying Taylor series in Calculus II.
The quadratic formula x = (-b ± sqrt(b^2 - 4ac))/(2a) sometimes produces a negative number under the square root. When the discriminant b^2 - 4ac is negative, the solutions are complex numbers.
For example, x^2 + 2x + 5 = 0 gives discriminant = 4 - 20 = -16. So x = (-2 ± sqrt(-16))/2 = (-2 ± 4i)/2 = -1 ± 2i. The solutions are x = -1 + 2i and x = -1 - 2i.

Notice the solutions come in conjugate pairs: a + bi and a - bi. This always happens when the polynomial has real coefficients. Complex roots always appear as conjugate pairs, so a real quadratic either has two real roots or two complex conjugate roots.

The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra says every polynomial of degree n has exactly n roots when you count complex roots and multiplicities. Complex numbers complete the number system so that every polynomial can be fully factored.
1) Simplify: (4 - 3i)(2 + i).
2) Find the modulus and argument of z = -3 - 3i.
3) Divide: (5 + i)/(2 - 3i). Write the result in a + bi form.
4) Solve x^2 - 4x + 13 = 0 and express the solutions in a + bi form.

Complex numbers might seem abstract, but they are indispensable in many fields. In electrical engineering, alternating current is described using complex numbers because voltage and current are sinusoidal waves — and Euler's formula makes calculations with sinusoids much easier.

In physics, quantum mechanics is built entirely on complex numbers. The wave function that describes a particle's state is complex-valued. Without complex numbers, there would be no modern understanding of atoms and particles.

Even in pure math, complex numbers solve problems that seem to have nothing to do with imaginary quantities. They complete the number system, guarantee all polynomials can be factored, and enable powerful techniques in analysis and number theory.
For calculus specifically, complex numbers help explain why certain real-valued integrals work out the way they do, and they connect exponential and trigonometric functions through Euler's formula.

Complex numbers extend the real number system by introducing i where i^2 = -1. Every complex number has the form a + bi with real part a and imaginary part b. Arithmetic follows normal algebra rules with i^2 = -1 applied at the end.

The complex plane plots these numbers with the real axis horizontal and imaginary axis vertical. Each complex number has a modulus (distance from origin) and argument (angle from positive real axis). Polar form z = r(cos theta + i·sin theta) simplifies multiplication, division, and powers via De Moivre's Theorem.

Complex conjugates (flip the sign of the imaginary part) are used for division and always appear as root pairs of real polynomials. Euler's formula e^(i·theta) = cos(theta) + i·sin(theta) unifies exponential and trig functions in a profound way.

chapters.title: Sequences and Series
chapters.description: Learn about ordered patterns of numbers, how to classify and sum them, and build the foundation for the infinite series you will study in Calculus II.

content_blocks.text: A sequence is an ordered list of numbers that follow a specific pattern or rule. Each number in the list is called a term. We label them a_1 (first term), a_2 (second term), a_3 (third term), and so on. The subscript tells you the position.

A sequence can be defined by a formula. For example, a_n = 2n + 1 generates the sequence 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, ... (plug in n = 1, 2, 3, ...). Or a sequence can be defined recursively, where each term depends on previous terms: a_1 = 1, a_n = a_(n-1) + 3 gives 1, 4, 7, 10, ...

We often ask whether a sequence converges or diverges. A sequence converges if its terms approach a specific number as n gets larger and larger. For example, a_n = 1/n gives 1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, ... which approaches 0. A sequence diverges if the terms do not settle on a single value.

The sequence a_n = (-1)^n gives -1, 1, -1, 1, ... This diverges because it keeps bouncing between -1 and 1 without settling. The sequence a_n = (n+1)/n gives 2, 3/2, 4/3, 5/4, ... which converges to 1.

An arithmetic sequence has a constant difference between consecutive terms. This difference is called the common difference, d. Each term is found by adding d to the previous term: a_n = a_1 + (n-1)·d.

For example, 5, 8, 11, 14, 17, ... is arithmetic with a_1 = 5 and d = 3. The nth term is a_n = 5 + (n-1)·3 = 3n + 2. You can use this formula to find any term directly without listing all the ones before it.

Arithmetic sequences correspond to linear functions. If you plot the terms (n, a_n), they lie on a straight line with slope d. This connection to linearity is why arithmetic sequences feel natural — they grow at a steady, constant rate.
Find the 50th term of the sequence 7, 12, 17, 22, ... Here a_1 = 7 and d = 5. a_50 = 7 + (50-1)·5 = 7 + 245 = 252.
A geometric sequence has a constant ratio between consecutive terms. This ratio is called the common ratio, r. Each term is found by multiplying the previous term by r: a_n = a_1 · r^(n-1).

For example, 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, ... is geometric with a_1 = 3 and r = 2. The nth term is a_n = 3 · 2^(n-1). Geometric sequences grow (or shrink) multiplicatively rather than additively, so they connect to exponential functions.

If |r| > 1, the terms grow without bound. If |r| < 1, the terms shrink toward zero. If r is negative, the terms alternate in sign. For instance, r = -1/2 gives a sequence like 8, -4, 2, -1, 1/2, ... which oscillates while shrinking.

slides.title: Worked Example: Identifying and Finding Sequence Terms

content_blocks.text: A sequence begins 4, 12, 36, 108, ... Is it arithmetic or geometric? Find the 10th term and a general formula.
Step 1: Check for arithmetic. Differences: 12 - 4 = 8, 36 - 12 = 24, 108 - 36 = 72. The differences are not constant, so it is not arithmetic.
Step 2: Check for geometric. Ratios: 12/4 = 3, 36/12 = 3, 108/36 = 3. The ratio is constant at r = 3, so it is geometric.
Step 3: Write the general formula. a_n = a_1 · r^(n-1) = 4 · 3^(n-1).

slides.title: Worked Example: Identifying and Finding Sequence Terms

content_blocks.text: A series is the sum of the terms of a sequence. If you have a sequence a_1, a_2, a_3, ..., the corresponding series is S = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + ... We often want to find the sum of a specific number of terms (a partial sum) or the total of all terms (if the series is infinite).
The notation S_n means the sum of the first n terms. For example, if the sequence is 2, 5, 8, 11, then S_4 = 2 + 5 + 8 + 11 = 26.

Sigma notation (also called summation notation) provides a compact way to write series. The expression ∑(k=1 to n) a_k means add up a_k for k = 1, 2, 3, ..., n. The letter k is the index of summation, and it takes on each integer value from the lower bound to the upper bound.
An arithmetic series is the sum of an arithmetic sequence. There is a simple formula: the sum of the first n terms is S_n = n/2 · (a_1 + a_n), or equivalently S_n = n/2 · (2a_1 + (n-1)d).

Why does this work? The idea comes from pairing terms. In the sum 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + 100, pair the first and last: 1 + 100 = 101. Pair the second and second-to-last: 2 + 99 = 101. There are 50 such pairs, so the total is 50 · 101 = 5050. This is the method Gauss reportedly discovered as a child.
The formula works for any arithmetic series. You just need a_1, a_n (or d), and n. The sum grows quadratically with n because you are adding more and more terms that are themselves getting larger.

A geometric series is the sum of a geometric sequence. The sum of the first n terms is: S_n = a_1 · (1 - r^n)/(1 - r), valid when r ≠ 1. This formula is derived by a clever trick: multiply S_n by r, then subtract to cancel most terms.

When |r| < 1, the terms get smaller and smaller. As n approaches infinity, r^n approaches 0, and the infinite geometric series has a finite sum: S = a_1 / (1 - r). This is your first example of an infinite process producing a finite result — a concept central to calculus.
When |r| >= 1, the terms do not shrink, and the infinite series diverges (the sum grows without bound or oscillates). So the condition |r| < 1 is critical for convergence.
Sum the infinite series 10 + 5 + 2.5 + 1.25 + ... Here a_1 = 10 and r = 1/2. Since |r| < 1, S = 10/(1 - 1/2) = 10/(1/2) = 20. The infinite sum is exactly 20.

slides.title: Worked Example: Sum of a Geometric Series

content_blocks.text: A ball is dropped from 6 feet. Each time it bounces, it reaches 2/3 of its previous height. Find the total vertical distance the ball travels (up and down combined) before coming to rest.

Step 1: The ball falls 6 feet initially. Then it bounces up 6·(2/3) = 4 feet and falls 4 feet. Then it bounces up 4·(2/3) = 8/3 feet and falls 8/3 feet. Each bounce adds both an up and a down distance.
Step 2: Total distance = 6 (initial drop) + 2·(4 + 8/3 + 16/9 + ...). The factor of 2 accounts for going up and coming back down on each bounce.

slides.title: Worked Example: Sum of a Geometric Series

content_blocks.text: Step 3: The bouncing heights form an infinite geometric series with a_1 = 4 and r = 2/3. Sum = 4/(1 - 2/3) = 4/(1/3) = 12.
Step 4: Total distance = 6 + 2·12 = 6 + 24 = 30 feet. The ball travels 30 feet total before stopping.

Sigma notation ∑ is the standard way to write sums compactly. It has three parts: the expression being summed (like k^2 or 3·(1/2)^k), the starting index (written below ∑, like k=1), and the ending index (written above ∑, like n or infinity).
You can manipulate sums using properties: ∑(k=1 to n) (a_k + b_k) = ∑ a_k + ∑ b_k. And ∑(k=1 to n) c·a_k = c · ∑ a_k. These let you break complex sums into simpler pieces.
Some useful closed-form sums to know: ∑(k=1 to n) k = n(n+1)/2. ∑(k=1 to n) k^2 = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6. ∑(k=1 to n) k^3 = [n(n+1)/2]^2. These formulas are used when computing Riemann sums in calculus.
Compute ∑(k=1 to 5) (2k - 1) = 1 + 3 + 5 + 7 + 9 = 25. Alternatively, using the formula: 2·∑k - ∑1 = 2·(5·6/2) - 5 = 30 - 5 = 25.

slides.title: Infinite Series and the Idea of Convergence

content_blocks.text: When we write an infinite series like ∑(k=1 to infinity) a_k, we are asking: does adding up all these terms give a finite number? If so, the series converges to that number. If not, it diverges.

Formally, we look at the partial sums S_n = a_1 + a_2 + ... + a_n. If the sequence of partial sums S_1, S_2, S_3, ... approaches a finite limit L as n goes to infinity, then the series converges to L. This is the same limit concept you will formalize in Calculus I.

slides.title: Infinite Series and the Idea of Convergence

content_blocks.text: A necessary condition for convergence is that the terms a_n must approach zero. If a_n does not go to zero, the series definitely diverges. But be careful: a_n going to zero is necessary, not sufficient. The harmonic series 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + ... has terms going to zero, but the sum still diverges to infinity.
In Calculus II, you will learn many tests to determine whether series converge or diverge. For now, just understand the core idea: convergence means the partial sums settle to a finite value.
1) Find a formula for the nth term of the sequence 2, 7, 12, 17, 22, ... and find a_30.
2) A geometric sequence has a_1 = 81 and r = 1/3. Find the 6th term.
3) Find the sum of the first 15 terms of the arithmetic series 3 + 7 + 11 + 15 + ...
4) Determine whether the infinite geometric series 12 + 4 + 4/3 + 4/9 + ... converges. If so, find the sum.

A sequence is an ordered list of numbers. Arithmetic sequences have a constant difference d between terms (a_n = a_1 + (n-1)d). Geometric sequences have a constant ratio r (a_n = a_1 · r^(n-1)). Sequences may converge to a limit or diverge.

A series is the sum of a sequence's terms. The arithmetic series sum is S_n = n/2 · (a_1 + a_n). The geometric series sum is S_n = a_1 · (1 - r^n)/(1 - r). Sigma notation provides compact shorthand for writing sums.

An infinite geometric series converges to a_1/(1 - r) when |r| < 1. This is one of the first examples of an infinite process producing a finite result. The concepts of convergence and divergence introduced here will be studied rigorously in Calculus I (limits) and Calculus II (infinite series).

chapters.title: Conic Sections and Analytic Geometry
chapters.description: Explore the equations and graphs of parabolas, ellipses, and hyperbolas. Strengthen your graphing skills and understand these curves that appear in physics, astronomy, and engineering.

content_blocks.text: Conic sections are the curves you get when you slice a double cone (two cones tip-to-tip) with a flat plane. Depending on the angle of the cut, you get a circle, an ellipse, a parabola, or a hyperbola. These four shapes have been studied for over 2000 years and appear throughout science and engineering.

A circle is the simplest conic — a perfectly round curve where every point is the same distance from the center. An ellipse is a stretched circle, like an oval. A parabola is an open curve with one branch (think of a satellite dish). A hyperbola has two separate branches that open in opposite directions.

Each conic section has a standard equation that describes its shape, and special points (like the center, focus, or vertex) that define its geometry. Understanding these equations lets you quickly sketch the curve and identify its key features.
You already met parabolas as graphs of quadratic functions. Now you will see them as part of a larger family of curves, and you will learn about ellipses and hyperbolas for the first time.
A circle is the set of all points at a fixed distance r (the radius) from a fixed point (h, k) called the center. Its standard equation is (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2.
When the center is at the origin, this simplifies to x^2 + y^2 = r^2. For example, x^2 + y^2 = 25 is a circle centered at (0, 0) with radius 5.

If the equation is given in expanded form like x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 4y - 12 = 0, you can convert to standard form by completing the square for both x and y. Group the x terms and y terms, complete the square for each, and you will reveal the center and radius.

Complete the square: x^2 - 6x + y^2 + 4y = 12. For x: (x^2 - 6x + 9) = (x-3)^2, add 9 to both sides. For y: (y^2 + 4y + 4) = (y+2)^2, add 4 to both sides. Result: (x-3)^2 + (y+2)^2 = 25. Center (3, -2), radius 5.

An ellipse looks like a stretched circle. It has two special points inside called foci (singular: focus). The defining property of an ellipse is: for any point on the ellipse, the sum of the distances to the two foci is constant.

The standard equation of an ellipse centered at the origin is x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1. Here a and b are the semi-axes. If a > b, the ellipse is wider than it is tall (the major axis is horizontal). If b > a, it is taller than wide (the major axis is vertical).

The major axis has length 2a (or 2b, whichever is larger) and connects the two vertices. The minor axis has length 2b (or 2a, whichever is smaller). The foci lie along the major axis at distance c from the center, where c^2 = a^2 - b^2 (assuming a > b).
x^2/25 + y^2/9 = 1 has a = 5, b = 3. Major axis is horizontal (since 5 > 3). c = sqrt(25 - 9) = sqrt(16) = 4. Foci at (4, 0) and (-4, 0). Vertices at (5, 0) and (-5, 0).

A hyperbola consists of two separate branches that open in opposite directions. Like an ellipse, it has two foci. But the defining property is different: for any point on the hyperbola, the difference of the distances to the two foci is constant.

The standard equation for a hyperbola centered at the origin opening left and right is x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1. For one opening up and down, it is y^2/a^2 - x^2/b^2 = 1. Notice the subtraction sign — that is what makes it a hyperbola instead of an ellipse.

Hyperbolas have asymptotes — lines that the branches approach but never touch. For x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1, the asymptotes are the lines y = (b/a)x and y = -(b/a)x. These asymptotes form an X through the center and guide the shape of the curve. The foci are at distance c from the center, where c^2 = a^2 + b^2.
x^2/16 - y^2/9 = 1 opens left-right. a = 4, b = 3. Asymptotes: y = (3/4)x and y = -(3/4)x. c = sqrt(16 + 9) = 5. Foci at (5, 0) and (-5, 0). Vertices at (4, 0) and (-4, 0).

slides.title: Worked Example: Identifying a Conic Section

content_blocks.text: Identify the conic section given by the equation 9x^2 + 4y^2 = 36. Find the center, vertices, and foci.
Step 1: Divide both sides by 36 to get standard form: x^2/4 + y^2/9 = 1.
Step 2: Both terms are positive with a plus sign and the right side is 1. This is an ellipse. Since 9 > 4, the larger denominator is under y^2, so the major axis is vertical.
Step 3: Read off the values. Under y^2: b^2 = 9, so b = 3. Under x^2: a^2 = 4, so a = 2. (Here we use the convention that b is the semi-major axis since b > a for this vertical ellipse.)

slides.title: Worked Example: Identifying a Conic Section

content_blocks.text: Step 4: Center is (0, 0). Vertices on the major axis: (0, 3) and (0, -3). c^2 = 9 - 4 = 5, so c = sqrt(5) ≈ 2.24. Foci at (0, sqrt(5)) and (0, -sqrt(5)).

You already know parabolas as graphs of quadratic functions. Now we look at them as conic sections with a geometric definition: a parabola is the set of all points equidistant from a fixed point (the focus) and a fixed line (the directrix).

For a parabola opening upward with vertex at the origin, the equation is x^2 = 4py, where p is the distance from the vertex to the focus. The focus is at (0, p) and the directrix is the horizontal line y = -p.
If the parabola opens rightward, the equation becomes y^2 = 4px, with focus at (p, 0) and directrix x = -p. Parabolas can open in any of the four directions depending on the equation form.
For x^2 = 12y, we have 4p = 12, so p = 3. The vertex is (0,0), the focus is (0, 3), and the directrix is y = -3. The parabola opens upward.

The focus-directrix property of parabolas is why satellite dishes and car headlights are parabolic — signals hitting the dish parallel to its axis all reflect to the focus, concentrating energy at one point.
All conic sections can be described by a single number called eccentricity, written e (not to be confused with Euler's number). Eccentricity measures how much the conic deviates from being a circle.

For a circle, e = 0 (perfectly round). For an ellipse, 0 < e < 1 (the closer to 0, the more circular). For a parabola, e = 1 exactly. For a hyperbola, e > 1. Eccentricity is computed as e = c/a for ellipses and hyperbolas.

This gives a unified view: as eccentricity increases from 0 past 1, you move from circles to ellipses to parabolas to hyperbolas. This unifying idea appears in orbital mechanics — planets orbit in ellipses, comets can follow parabolic or hyperbolic paths.
Earth's orbit has eccentricity about 0.017 (very close to a circle). A comet on a one-time visit might have e = 1.2 (hyperbolic — it flies by the sun and never returns).
Analytic geometry uses algebra to study geometric shapes on the coordinate plane. Two of the most fundamental tools are the distance formula and the midpoint formula.

The distance between two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is d = sqrt((x2 - x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2). This comes directly from the Pythagorean theorem: the horizontal and vertical differences form the legs of a right triangle, and the distance is the hypotenuse.
The midpoint of a line segment connecting (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is ((x1 + x2)/2, (y1 + y2)/2). You simply average the x-coordinates and average the y-coordinates.
Find the distance and midpoint between (1, 3) and (7, 11). Distance: sqrt((7-1)^2 + (11-3)^2) = sqrt(36 + 64) = sqrt(100) = 10. Midpoint: ((1+7)/2, (3+11)/2) = (4, 7).
Graph the hyperbola given by (y - 1)^2/4 - (x + 2)^2/9 = 1. Identify the center, vertices, foci, and asymptotes.
Step 1: Identify the center. The equation is in standard form with center (h, k) = (-2, 1), read from (x - (-2)) and (y - 1).
Step 2: Determine orientation. The positive term is (y - 1)^2, so the hyperbola opens up and down (vertical transverse axis). a^2 = 4 so a = 2, b^2 = 9 so b = 3.
Step 3: Find vertices. They are a units above and below the center: (-2, 1+2) = (-2, 3) and (-2, 1-2) = (-2, -1).
Step 4: Find foci. c^2 = a^2 + b^2 = 4 + 9 = 13, so c = sqrt(13) ≈ 3.61. Foci at (-2, 1 + sqrt(13)) and (-2, 1 - sqrt(13)).
Step 5: Asymptotes. For a vertical hyperbola centered at (h, k): y - k = ±(a/b)(x - h). So y - 1 = ±(2/3)(x + 2), or y = (2/3)x + 7/3 and y = -(2/3)x - 1/3.
Any conic section can be described by the general second-degree equation Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0. You can determine which conic it is by looking at the coefficients.

When B = 0 (no xy term): if A = C, it is a circle. If A and C have the same sign but are different, it is an ellipse. If A or C is zero (but not both), it is a parabola. If A and C have opposite signs, it is a hyperbola.

When there is a B term (Bxy), the conic is rotated. The discriminant B^2 - 4AC determines the type: negative means ellipse or circle, zero means parabola, positive means hyperbola. Rotation of conics is an advanced topic, but knowing the discriminant test is useful.
Classify 3x^2 - 5y^2 + 6x + 20y - 7 = 0. Here A = 3, B = 0, C = -5. A and C have opposite signs, so this is a hyperbola.
1) Write the equation of a circle with center (-1, 4) and radius 6.
2) For the ellipse x^2/49 + y^2/16 = 1, find the vertices, co-vertices, and foci.
3) Find the vertex, focus, and directrix of the parabola y^2 = -8x.
4) Classify the conic: 4x^2 + 4y^2 - 16x + 8y + 11 = 0. Rewrite in standard form.

Conic sections — circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas — arise from slicing a cone. Each has a standard equation and key features (center, vertices, foci, asymptotes, directrix) that define its shape.
Circles: (x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2. Ellipses: x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 with c^2 = a^2 - b^2. Parabolas: x^2 = 4py (or y^2 = 4px). Hyperbolas: x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 with c^2 = a^2 + b^2.

Eccentricity unifies conics: e = 0 for circles, 0 < e < 1 for ellipses, e = 1 for parabolas, e > 1 for hyperbolas. The distance and midpoint formulas are fundamental tools. Conics appear in planetary orbits, satellite dishes, bridge arches, and many other real-world applications.

chapters.title: Vectors and Parametric Equations
chapters.description: Explore vectors in the plane, learn vector operations, and discover how parametric equations describe curves and motion — essential tools for multivariable calculus.

content_blocks.text: A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude (size) and direction. Think of it as an arrow: the length of the arrow represents how much, and the direction the arrow points tells you which way.

Examples of vectors in real life: velocity (speed and direction of movement), force (strength and direction of a push), and displacement (how far and in what direction you moved). Compare this to a scalar, which has only magnitude — like temperature, mass, or speed without direction.

We write vectors using component form: v = <a, b> means the vector points a units in the x-direction and b units in the y-direction. The vector <3, 4> starts at the origin (by convention) and ends at the point (3, 4). Vectors can be placed anywhere — only the magnitude and direction matter, not the starting point.
A car drives 5 miles east and 2 miles north. Its displacement vector is <5, 2>. The magnitude (distance traveled in a straight line) is sqrt(5^2 + 2^2) = sqrt(29) ≈ 5.39 miles.

slides.title: Vector Operations: Addition and Scalar Multiplication

content_blocks.text: Vector addition combines two vectors by adding their components. If u = <a, b> and v = <c, d>, then u + v = <a + c, b + d>. Geometrically, this is the 'tip-to-tail' method: place the tail of v at the tip of u, and the sum is the arrow from the start of u to the tip of v.
Scalar multiplication scales a vector. If k is a number and v = <a, b>, then k·v = <ka, kb>. If k > 1, the vector stretches. If 0 < k < 1, it shrinks. If k < 0, it flips direction and scales.
Vector subtraction is u - v = u + (-v) = <a - c, b - d>. Geometrically, u - v points from the tip of v to the tip of u (when both start at the same point).
The magnitude (or length) of a vector v = <a, b> is |v| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2). This is just the distance formula applied to the vector's components. For v = <3, 4>, |v| = sqrt(9 + 16) = 5.
A unit vector is a vector with magnitude 1. It indicates direction only, with no scaling. To find the unit vector in the direction of v, divide by its magnitude: u = v / |v| = <a/|v|, b/|v|>.

Two special unit vectors have their own names: i = <1, 0> (pointing in the positive x-direction) and j = <0, 1> (pointing in the positive y-direction). Any vector <a, b> can be written as a·i + b·j. For example, <3, -2> = 3i - 2j.
Find the unit vector in the direction of <-5, 12>. Magnitude: sqrt(25 + 144) = sqrt(169) = 13. Unit vector: <-5/13, 12/13>.

The dot product (also called the scalar product) takes two vectors and produces a single number. For u = <a, b> and v = <c, d>, the dot product is u · v = ac + bd. Multiply corresponding components and add.

The dot product has a beautiful geometric meaning: u · v = |u| · |v| · cos(theta), where theta is the angle between the two vectors. This lets you find the angle between vectors: cos(theta) = (u · v) / (|u| · |v|).

Two vectors are perpendicular (orthogonal) when the angle between them is 90 degrees, which means cos(90°) = 0. So u and v are perpendicular if and only if u · v = 0. This is a quick test for perpendicularity.
u = <2, 3> and v = <6, -4>. Dot product: 2(6) + 3(-4) = 12 - 12 = 0. Since the dot product is zero, u and v are perpendicular.

slides.title: Worked Example: Finding the Angle Between Vectors

content_blocks.text: Find the angle between u = <1, 3> and v = <4, 2>.
Step 1: Compute the dot product. u · v = 1(4) + 3(2) = 4 + 6 = 10.
Step 2: Find the magnitudes. |u| = sqrt(1 + 9) = sqrt(10). |v| = sqrt(16 + 4) = sqrt(20) = 2·sqrt(5).
Step 3: Use the angle formula. cos(theta) = 10 / (sqrt(10) · 2·sqrt(5)) = 10 / (2·sqrt(50)) = 10 / (2 · 5·sqrt(2)) = 10 / (10·sqrt(2)) = 1/sqrt(2).

slides.title: Worked Example: Finding the Angle Between Vectors

content_blocks.text: Step 4: Solve for theta. cos(theta) = 1/sqrt(2) = sqrt(2)/2. So theta = pi/4 = 45 degrees. The vectors are 45 degrees apart.

Normally we describe a curve as y = f(x). But some curves are hard or impossible to write this way — for example, a circle (which fails the vertical line test) or the path of a projectile where we also want to track time.

Parametric equations solve this by expressing both x and y as functions of a third variable, usually t (often representing time): x = f(t) and y = g(t). As t varies over some interval, the point (x, y) traces out a curve in the plane.

The parameter t adds extra information: it tells you not just where the curve goes, but when the moving point reaches each location and in what direction it travels. This makes parametric equations ideal for describing motion.
x = cos(t) and y = sin(t) for t in [0, 2·pi] traces out the unit circle counterclockwise. At t = 0 the point is (1, 0), at t = pi/2 it is (0, 1), at t = pi it is (-1, 0), and so on.
To graph a parametric curve, make a table of t values. For each t, compute x and y, then plot the points (x, y). Connect them in order of increasing t, using arrows to show direction.

Sometimes you can eliminate the parameter to get a Cartesian equation. For x = t + 1 and y = t^2, solve the first for t: t = x - 1. Substitute into the second: y = (x - 1)^2. This is a parabola. But note that t might be restricted (say t >= 0), which restricts what part of the parabola is traced.

Elimination is useful for understanding the shape, but the parametric form often carries more information — direction of travel, speed, and timing. In calculus, you will compute derivatives and integrals with respect to the parameter directly.
x = 3·cos(t), y = 2·sin(t). Eliminate t: cos(t) = x/3 and sin(t) = y/2. Using cos^2 + sin^2 = 1: x^2/9 + y^2/4 = 1. This is an ellipse.

slides.title: Worked Example: Parametric Equations of a Projectile

content_blocks.text: A ball is launched at 30 m/s at an angle of 60° above the horizontal. Write parametric equations for its position (ignoring air resistance) and find how far it travels horizontally before hitting the ground.
Step 1: Find initial velocity components. v_x = 30·cos(60°) = 30·(1/2) = 15 m/s. v_y = 30·sin(60°) = 30·(sqrt(3)/2) = 15·sqrt(3) ≈ 25.98 m/s.
Step 2: Write parametric equations. x(t) = 15t (constant horizontal velocity, no air resistance). y(t) = 15·sqrt(3)·t - (1/2)(9.8)t^2 = 15·sqrt(3)·t - 4.9t^2.

slides.title: Worked Example: Parametric Equations of a Projectile

content_blocks.text: Step 3: Find when the ball hits the ground. Set y = 0: t(15·sqrt(3) - 4.9t) = 0. So t = 0 (launch) or t = 15·sqrt(3)/4.9 ≈ 25.98/4.9 ≈ 5.30 seconds.
Step 4: Find horizontal distance. x(5.30) = 15 · 5.30 ≈ 79.5 meters. The ball lands about 79.5 meters away.

Vectors and parametric equations are closely related. A vector-valued function r(t) = <x(t), y(t)> assigns a position vector to each value of t. As t changes, the tip of the vector traces out a curve — this is exactly a parametric curve.

The velocity vector is the derivative of the position vector with respect to t: v(t) = <x'(t), y'(t)>. Its direction tells which way the object is moving, and its magnitude |v(t)| gives the speed. This is a preview of how calculus handles motion in the plane.

A simple but important example: a line through point P = (x0, y0) in the direction of vector d = <a, b> can be parametrized as r(t) = <x0 + at, y0 + bt>. Here t = 0 gives the starting point, and increasing t moves you along the line in the direction of d.
The line through (2, -1) in the direction <3, 4> has parametric equations x = 2 + 3t, y = -1 + 4t. At t = 0: (2, -1). At t = 1: (5, 3). At t = -1: (-1, -5).
1) Given u = <-3, 7> and v = <2, 4>, find u + v, u - v, 2u, and u · v.
2) Find the unit vector in the direction of <5, -12>.
3) Eliminate the parameter: x = t^2, y = 2t + 1. What curve does this describe?
4) Write parametric equations for an ellipse centered at the origin with semi-axes a = 4 and b = 2.

slides.title: Why Vectors and Parametrics Matter for Calculus

content_blocks.text: In Calculus II, you will compute arc lengths and areas using parametric curves. You will also work with polar coordinates, which describe curves using angles and distances — a natural fit for parametric representation.

In Calculus III (multivariable), vectors become central. You will use vector-valued functions to describe curves in 3D space, compute tangent and normal vectors, and study the motion of objects along space curves. Concepts like the dot product extend to 3D and lead to the cross product.

Line integrals, surface integrals, and the major theorems of vector calculus (Green's, Stokes', Divergence) all depend on parametric descriptions of curves and surfaces. The foundations you are building now will be used throughout advanced math.

slides.title: Why Vectors and Parametrics Matter for Calculus

content_blocks.text: If vectors feel abstract now, remember that every physical quantity involving direction — wind, ocean currents, gravity, electric fields — is best described with vectors. Learning vectors now is investing in a language you will use constantly.

slides.title: Chapter Summary: Vectors and Parametric Equations

content_blocks.text: Vectors have magnitude and direction. They are written in component form <a, b> and support addition, scalar multiplication, and the dot product. The dot product u · v = |u||v|cos(theta) connects algebra and geometry and tests for perpendicularity.

Parametric equations x = f(t), y = g(t) describe curves by expressing both coordinates as functions of a parameter. They handle curves that are not functions, describe motion with timing, and can be converted to Cartesian form by eliminating the parameter.
Together, vectors and parametric equations form the language of motion and multidimensional geometry. They are essential for studying curves, surfaces, and physical quantities in Calculus II and III.
